title: Cell division — how cells copy themselves (mitosis)
examTip: Mitosis questions appear on every AQA Biology paper. Know why cells divide, what mitosis produces, and what goes wrong in cancer. The cell cycle and the term 'cytokinesis' are also fair game.
introTips: You're made of around 37 trillion cells — and every single one of them came from one original cell dividing over and over. Mitosis is how that works.
You'll learn what actually happens when a cell copies itself, why the chromosome number has to be maintained at 46, and what happens when the process goes wrong.
This is also the foundation for understanding meiosis — so getting mitosis solid first will make the next lesson much easier.
intro: Right now, somewhere in your body, cells are dividing. Your skin replaces itself every few weeks. Your red blood cells last only 120 days before new ones are made. Every time this happens, the cell has to copy its entire instruction manual — all 46 chromosomes — and then split into two perfect copies of itself. That process is called mitosis. Get it right and you grow and heal. Get it wrong and cells can start dividing without stopping — which is what cancer is.

keyPoints.heading: Why cells divide — growth, repair and replacement
keyPoints.content: Your body needs cell division for three reasons: growth (turning a fertilised egg into a full-sized person), repair (healing a cut or a broken bone), and replacement (swapping out worn-out cells like skin or red blood cells). Every new cell made by mitosis is genetically identical to the original — same 46 chromosomes, same DNA sequence. That's essential. If a replacement liver cell had different DNA, it wouldn't work. Mitosis keeps the instruction set the same.
writeBullets: Cells divide for: growth, repair, and replacement
Mitosis produces two daughter cells genetically identical to the parent
Both daughter cells have 46 chromosomes (diploid) — the same as the parent
Identical DNA is essential so new cells can do exactly the same job

keyTerms.def: A type of cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with 46 chromosomes
A cell containing two sets of chromosomes — 46 in humans
A new cell produced by cell division

keyPoints.memTip: Three reasons for mitosis — GRR: Growth, Repair, Replacement. Say it out loud three times. Then cover this and write the three uses from memory.

keyPoints.heading: What actually happens in mitosis
keyPoints.content: Before a cell can divide, it has to duplicate all its DNA so each daughter cell gets a full set. The sequence goes like this: first, DNA replication — every chromosome is copied so the cell now has 92 chromosomes arranged in 46 identical pairs. Then the chromosomes line up down the middle of the cell. Then the cell divides — one complete set of 46 chromosomes goes to each side. The result: two identical daughter cells, each with 46 chromosomes. AQA does not require you to name the individual stages (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase) for GCSE — but you do need to describe what happens.
writeBullets: DNA replicates first: 46 chromosomes → 92 (each chromosome is copied)
Chromosomes line up in the middle of the cell
Cell divides: one set of 46 chromosomes goes to each new cell
Result: 2 daughter cells, each genetically identical, each with 46 chromosomes
AQA GCSE does NOT require naming PMAT stages — describe what happens instead

keyTerms.def: The process by which a cell copies all its DNA before dividing
A thread-like structure made of DNA that carries genetic information; humans have 46

keyPoints.memTip: Three steps: Copy → Line up → Split. Write those three words and draw a quick sketch: one cell with 46 chromosomes, copies to 92, lines up, splits into two cells each with 46. If you can draw it, you can explain it.

keyPoints.heading: The cell cycle — three phases, one complete loop
keyPoints.content: Mitosis is just one part of a bigger loop called the cell cycle. Most of the time, cells are not actually dividing — they're in interphase, which is the preparation phase. During interphase the cell grows larger, copies its DNA, and makes extra cell components ready for division. Then comes mitosis itself — the nucleus divides and the chromosomes are sorted into two sets. Finally, cytokinesis splits the cytoplasm to produce two fully separate daughter cells. Then the whole cycle starts again. A typical human cell takes around 24 hours to complete one full cycle.
writeBullets: Interphase: cell grows, DNA replicates, organelles copied — takes up most of the cycle
Mitosis: nucleus divides, chromosomes sorted into two identical sets
Cytokinesis: cytoplasm splits → two separate daughter cells
The cycle then repeats in both new daughter cells

keyTerms.def: The sequence of events a cell goes through to grow and divide: interphase → mitosis → cytokinesis
The phase between divisions — the cell grows, replicates its DNA and prepares for mitosis
The splitting of the cytoplasm to produce two separate daughter cells after mitosis

keyPoints.memTip: I — M — C: Interphase, Mitosis, Cytokinesis. Think of it as a clock face. Most of the clock is interphase (preparation). A short arc is mitosis. A tiny notch is cytokinesis. Write it as a circle with labels.

keyPoints.heading: Cancer — when the off-switch breaks
keyPoints.content: Normally, cell division is tightly controlled — cells only divide when the body needs them to. But sometimes a mutation in the DNA damages the genes that control cell division. The cell loses the ability to stop dividing. It keeps copying itself over and over, producing a mass of cells that don't do any useful job. This mass is called a tumour. If the tumour grows in one place and doesn't spread, it's called benign. If the cells break off and invade other tissues, it's malignant — this is what we call cancer. Risk factors include smoking, UV radiation, and some viruses, because these all increase the chance of mutations occurring.
writeBullets: Cancer = uncontrolled cell division caused by mutations in genes that control the cell cycle
Tumour = a mass of cells produced by uncontrolled division
Benign tumour: stays in one place, doesn't invade other tissues
Malignant tumour: cells spread to other parts of the body — this is cancer
Risk factors: smoking, UV radiation, some viruses (all increase mutation risk)

keyTerms.def: A change in the DNA sequence — can disrupt normal cell cycle control
A mass of cells formed by uncontrolled cell division
A tumour that stays in one place and does not spread
A tumour whose cells invade other tissues — cancer

keyPoints.memTip: Cancer is a broken off-switch — the mutation removes the signal that tells a cell to stop dividing. Cover this section and explain in one sentence what a mutation does to the cell cycle. Then explain the difference between benign and malignant without looking.

tables.title: The cell cycle phases
rows: Cell grows; DNA replicates; organelles copied; preparations for division
Nuclear division: chromosomes lined up and separated into two identical sets
Cytoplasm splits to form two separate daughter cells

1.question: Which three things does mitosis allow the body to do?
options: Grow, repair, and replace worn-out cells
Replace cells, produce gametes, and grow
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — growth, repair, and replacement. These are the three uses of mitosis you need to know.
1.wrongFeedback: Mitosis is for growth, repair, and replacement of worn-out cells. It does NOT produce gametes (that's meiosis) and it's not specifically for antibody production.

3.question: What is cytokinesis?
options: The replication of DNA before cell division
The stage where chromosomes line up in the middle of the cell
The splitting of the cytoplasm to produce two separate daughter cells
A mutation that causes uncontrolled cell division
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — cytokinesis is the splitting of the cytoplasm, the final step that actually creates two physically separate cells.
3.wrongFeedback: Cytokinesis is the very last step of the cell cycle — it's when the cytoplasm physically splits to create two daughter cells. DNA replication happens in interphase. Chromosomes lining up is part of mitosis itself.

commonMistakes: Saying mitosis produces cells with 23 chromosomes — that's meiosis. Mitosis always produces two cells each with 46 chromosomes, identical to the parent.
Describing cancer as 'cells dividing faster than normal' — the key point is that division is uncontrolled, not just fast. Mutations remove the stop signal.
Confusing interphase with 'resting' — cells in interphase are extremely active. DNA is being replicated and the cell is growing. It's the preparation phase, not a rest.
Mixing up benign and malignant — benign stays put (not cancerous), malignant spreads to other tissues (cancerous). Remember: Malignant = spreads.
Saying mitosis is for 'reproduction' — in humans, mitosis is for growth, repair and replacement of body cells only. Sexual reproduction uses meiosis to make gametes.
examTips: If a question asks how many chromosomes are in a daughter cell after mitosis — always answer 46 (same as the parent). If you see 23, that's meiosis.
For cancer questions: make sure you mention both (1) a mutation occurs in the genes controlling cell division, and (2) cells divide uncontrollably. AQA often wants both ideas for full marks.
On cell cycle diagrams: interphase takes up the largest proportion of the cycle. If asked to label a pie chart, interphase should be the biggest segment.

videoLinks.searchQuery: AQA GCSE biology mitosis cell cycle
videoLinks.label: Mitosis and the Cell Cycle

revisionCardBullets: Mitosis = cell division that produces 2 genetically IDENTICAL daughter cells, each with 46 chromosomes (diploid)
Three uses of mitosis: Growth, Repair, Replacement (GRR)
Sequence: DNA replicates (46→92) → chromosomes line up → cell divides → 2 identical cells each with 46
AQA GCSE does NOT require you to name PMAT stages — describe what happens instead
Cell cycle = Interphase (grow + replicate DNA) → Mitosis (nucleus divides) → Cytokinesis (cytoplasm splits)
Interphase takes up the majority of the cell cycle — the cell is actively growing and copying DNA
Cytokinesis = splitting of the cytoplasm — the final step, producing two separate cells
Cancer = uncontrolled cell division caused by mutations damaging cell-cycle control genes
Tumour = mass of cells from uncontrolled division | Benign = stays put | Malignant = spreads (cancer)
